behaviors.description: Speech-to-text output preserves speaker labels so attendees can be attributed specific statements
behaviors.justification: Speaker label preservation depends on correctly handling the real transcription API's output format. A pure Unit mock could return speaker labels in any format; the real API returns them in a specific structure (nested JSON, specific field names, metadata schema). Testing with realistic API response data (recorded or from a test endpoint) is necessary to catch format mismatches or missing fields in our parsing logic that would cause labels to be lost or misattributed. This is a data contract issue that requires Integration testing.
behaviors.test_description: Create an integration test using a recorded speech-to-text API response that includes speaker labels in the real API's format. Process this through the transcription parsing layer and verify: (1) all speaker labels are extracted and stored in the transcript object, (2) each speaker is mapped to their statements, (3) speaker attribution flows through to the final summary with attendee names linked to their contributions.

behaviors.description: Structured summary contains at least one action item for every attendee assigned a task
behaviors.justification: The final output depends on LLM behavior: whether the model recognizes which attendees were assigned which tasks and extracts them consistently. This is non-deterministic and cannot be reliably mocked. A Unit/Integration test with a mock LLM always produces perfect action items for all attendees, hiding the real failure: 'the actual LLM missed attendee X's task.' To catch both LLM extraction failures and code parsing failures, we need the real LLM attempting extraction against realistic meeting transcripts.
behaviors.test_description: Run the real LLM summarizer on a meeting transcript where three attendees (Alice, Bob, Carol) are explicitly assigned distinct tasks with clear responsibility language. Call the actual summarization API and verify the structured output contains an 'actionItems' array where each attendee appears with at least one associated action item. This simultaneously tests LLM extraction behavior (did it find all tasks?) and code parsing logic (did we correctly extract and structure what the LLM found?).

behaviors.description: Key decisions appear in the 'decisions' section, not buried in narrative or other fields
behaviors.justification: Whether decisions end up in the correct output field depends on LLM prompt adherence and output structure compliance. A mock LLM will always produce the expected schema; the real LLM might ignore field placement instructions, output decisions inline with narrative text, use different field names, or format decisions inconsistently. This is a real failure mode—LLM deviation from schema. Only actual LLM output reveals this.
behaviors.test_description: Run the real LLM summarizer on a meeting transcript containing at least three explicit decision statements (e.g., 'We've decided to migrate to vendor X', 'The project timeline is set to Q3'). Verify the structured summary output contains a populated 'decisions' field with those decisions extracted. Verify field names match the schema exactly (not variations like 'decided_items' or 'key_decisions'). Spot-check that narrative sections do not duplicate decision content, confirming decisions were moved to the decisions field, not copied.

behaviors.description: Long audio (>60 min) is split into segments, processed sequentially, and merged into coherent summary without data loss
behaviors.justification: This behavior requires both technical coordination (file splitting, sequential processing) and LLM-dependent coherence (merged segments summarize without gaps, contradictions, or duplication). A System test would catch file handling bugs but might pass even if the merged summary is incoherent. An Agentic test that runs the real LLM on merged segment transcripts is necessary to verify the final summary accurately reflects the complete meeting arc. The coherence requirement is the binding constraint.
behaviors.test_description: Create a realistic 90-minute meeting transcript representing three 30-minute segments (e.g., problem discussion → solution design → implementation planning). Verify the system detects >60 min and splits into segments. Transcribe and summarize each segment independently, then merge all segment transcripts. Run the real LLM summarizer on the merged transcript. Verify: (1) no content from any segment is missing from the final summary, (2) no content is duplicated across summaries, (3) the final summary coherently tells the story of the full 90-minute meeting without contradictions or discontinuities (e.g., a decision mentioned in segment 1 is still referenced correctly in the final summary for segment 3).

behaviors.description: Full pipeline (upload, transcribe, summarize, deliver) completes end-to-end without manual intervention on real meeting recordings
behaviors.justification: This behavior explicitly requires testing the complete pipeline. Individual component tests (Unit/Integration/System/Agentic) could pass while the pipeline fails due to integration failures: mismatched data types between steps, missing error handling, sequencing deadlocks, timeout cascades, or missing cleanup logic. The real failure—'the pipeline stalls or crashes at step X'—can only be detected by running the full workflow with real inputs. This is a coordination and stability test, not a single-component test.
behaviors.test_description: Obtain two real meeting recordings: one <30 min and one 60+ min. Submit each through the complete pipeline without intervention: (1) upload audio file, (2) await transcription with speaker labels, (3) await LLM summarization, (4) verify structured output is delivered with correct schema (populated summary, actionItems, decisions, attendees sections). Verify no step requires manual prompting, correction, or callback. Measure pipeline latency and verify all steps complete within expected timeouts. Run both recordings to verify segmentation path (short) and merging path (long) both complete autonomously.
